title: Needle-felted jay
subtitle: A UK woodland bird with its distinctive blue wing patch
excerpt: Felt a plump jay body in pinkish-buff merino with a bold blue and black wing patch and a streaked black moustache. The vivid blue wing covert is the signature detail of the piece and makes an immediately recognisable result.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting guild practice. The jay is a popular subject in UK woodland-bird felting series because its distinctive blue wing patch provides a clear colourwork exercise.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry technique for sculpting wool by stabbing repeatedly with barbed needles. The barbs on the needle catch and interlock the wool fibres with each pass, building a firm, stable shape.

content.text: The jay's colouring is distinctive: a pale pinkish-brown body, black tail, white rump, and a small patch of bright barred blue on each wing. This pattern focuses on the body shape and the blue wing patch, which is the key colourwork exercise. A jay at this scale is about 9 cm long from bill to tail.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Wear a finger guard on the hand holding the work.

items.name: Merino roving, pinkish-buff or pale fawn (body)
Merino roving, cobalt or sky blue (wing patch)
Merino roving, black (wing, tail, moustache stripe)
Merino roving, white or cream (wing bar, rump)

content.text: Fold 12 g of buff merino into a compact oval about 6 cm long and 3.5 cm wide. Needle all over with the size-36 needle to compress and firm the core. Rotate constantly. The body should feel solid and resist squeezing after 8 to 10 minutes of work.

Roll 4 g of buff merino into a round ball about 2.5 cm across. Needle to firmness, then attach to the front of the body with bridging wisps of merino needled across the join. The head should sit slightly above the body centreline, not directly on top.

Needle a flat rectangle of black merino about 5 cm long and 2 cm wide. Attach it to the rear of the body, angling it slightly upward. Add a thin white patch just above the tail base to suggest the white rump.

The wing covert patch is the defining feature. Needle a small oval of black merino onto each side of the body at mid-point to represent the primary feathers. On top of each black patch, needle a smaller oval of blue merino. Over the blue, needle two thin horizontal lines of white to suggest the barring on the wing coverts. Use the size-40 needle for the fine barring lines.

Needle a thin black stripe from the base of the beak downward on each side of the face to suggest the moustachial stripe. Add a tiny black bill and two tiny black eye dots. Go over the whole surface with the size-40 needle in light passes to finish the

items.problem: The blue wing patch keeps blending into the buff body
items.cause: The buff body surface was not felted firmly enough before the blue was applied
items.fix: Before adding any colour patches, the body surface should be dense enough that needling the size-40 into it produces no loose fibres. If the surface is too open, the blue will migrate into the buff. Add a thin flat layer of buff merino over the body first and needle until fully smooth, then apply the coloured patches.
